Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Comparative simile phrase with adjectival force
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Refers to someone extremely pale, weak, or emotionally devastated.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A hmel a vangin a chau hle.
Usage Contexts: Daily speech, family discussions, illness narratives
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from traditional visual associations with death and mourning in Mizo society.
First Example Sentence: Naupang chu natna avângin mitthi thlâk ang mai a ni.
Second Example Sentence: Exam a tlin loh hnuah a hmêl chu mitthi thlâk ang a lo ni ta.
Third Example Sentence: Ramri khawihna thu an hre chuan mipuite chu mitthi thlâk ang an lo awm.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase with abstract noun complement
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Describes becoming deeply discouraged or emotionally burdened.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A rilru a buaiin a lawm lo.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Thinlung in Mizo culture symbolizes both emotion and moral resilience.
First Example Sentence: Hna tam lutuk avângin thinlung sâwt a ti hle.
Second Example Sentence: A ṭhianin a hnuk chiah avângin a thinlung a sâwt ta.
Third Example Sentence: Khawtlâng harsatna hmuin zirtirtu chu thinlung sâwt takin a awm.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verbal idiom with directional metaphor
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Used when everyone suddenly pays attention to one issue or person.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Mi zawng zawngin an ngai pawimawh ta.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects communal attention patterns in tightly connected hill communities.
First Example Sentence: Thuthang thar chu khawvêl hawi vek a lo ni.
Second Example Sentence: A inchhuih dân avângin election chu khawvêl hawi vek a ni ta.
Third Example Sentence: Mizo film thar pakhatin social media chu khawvêl hawi vek a siam.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Refers to something beautiful but fragile or temporary.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: A mawi mahse rei tak a awm lo.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The bâwngbel flower is admired in oral literature for delicate beauty.
First Example Sentence: An hmangaihna chu bâwngbel par ang a ni.
Second Example Sentence: Tlangram nuam tak chu bâwngbel par angin a ral nhanh.
Third Example Sentence: Naupang lenna lawmna chu bâwngbel par ang mai a lo awm.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Describes being under intense pressure or danger.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Fire imagery is common in Mizo oral narratives to symbolize imminent danger.
First Example Sentence: Exam hnaih avângin zirlai tam tak chu lu chungah mei al ang an awm.
Second Example Sentence: Debt nasa tak nei chuan lu chungah mei al ang a ni.
Third Example Sentence: Ram buaina avângin mipuite chu lu chungah mei al angin an awm ta.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Means experiencing relief after prolonged hardship.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Kawrthah is a dry season month; finding water symbolizes unexpected relief.
First Example Sentence: Hna thar a hmuh chu kawrthah tui hmu ang a ni.
Second Example Sentence: Doctor tha an hmu ta chu kawrthah tui hmu ang an ti.
Third Example Sentence: Loan an dawn hnuah chhungkua chu kawrthah tui hmu ang an lawm.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Describes someone overly timid or easily frightened.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Wild boars in hill regions are known for unpredictable fear reactions.
First Example Sentence: A naupang chu vawk tlâng hlau ang mai a ni.
Second Example Sentence: Interview hmaah vawk tlâng hlau angin a inthlahdah.
Third Example Sentence: Mithiam tak pawhin mipui hmaah vawk tlâng hlau ang a ni fo.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Means mentally shutting oneself off from others or ideas.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Frequently used in church and educational discourse encouraging openness.
First Example Sentence: A rilru a inkhâr avângin mi dang thu a ngai lo.
Second Example Sentence: Zirtirtuin zirlaite rilru inkhâr lo tûrin a fuih.
Third Example Sentence: Politics lama rilru inkhâr chu inpumkhatna tibuaitu a ni.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Used when people blindly follow a trend or majority opinion.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Mi dang zui mai a ni.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Directional imagery reflects communal movement across mountain settlements.
First Example Sentence: Social media trend chu thlang lam thli vek ang an zui.
Second Example Sentence: Mipui tam zawk thu chu ngaihtuah lovin thlang lam thli vekin an kal.
Third Example Sentence: Election campaign-ah mi tam tak chu thlang lam thli vek mai an ni.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Describes someone naive despite living in an advanced or urban environment.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects contrasts between village wisdom and modern urban living in Mizoram.
First Example Sentence: Aizawl-ah rei tak awm mahse khawpui mitdel ang a la ni.
Second Example Sentence: Technology hman thiam lo chu an khawpui mitdel ti fo.
Third Example Sentence: Market-ah hralh thiam loh avângin khawpui mitdel angin a lang.
